title: A Dispute About Who Gets the Credit
summary: Two researchers dispute attribution on a shared paper, navigating questions of intellectual contribution, academic convention, and what fair credit actually means in collaborative work.

body.speaker: Researcher A
body.text: I need to raise this now, before the submission deadline. The authorship order on the paper — I can't be listed as second author. The methodology you're presenting was mine. I developed it over the two years before you joined the project.

body.speaker: Researcher B
body.text: I understand why you feel that way. But authorship order in our field reflects contribution to the paper as submitted, not to the project history. The analysis in the final draft — the three additional models, the revised framing, the discussion section — those were my work. That's the contribution that matters for publication.

body.speaker: Researcher A
body.text: That framing only made sense because of the methodology underneath it. If you remove my foundational work, there is no paper. You're treating the visible contribution — the writing, the reframing — as if it's more significant than the intellectual groundwork that made it possible.

body.speaker: Researcher B
body.text: I'm not dismissing the foundational work. But the methodology had existed for two years without producing a publishable paper. What turned it into one was the analytical approach I developed and the way I repositioned the argument for the journal. That's not a minor editorial contribution.

body.speaker: Researcher A
body.text: I accept that your contribution to the final draft was substantial. What I'm contesting is the principle. In our institution's authorship guidelines, the person who conceived the core intellectual framework is listed first, regardless of who wrote the most in the final document.

body.speaker: Researcher B
body.text: I've read those guidelines too, and I read them differently. The 'core intellectual framework' of this paper is the three-way comparison I designed. The original methodology is a tool within that framework, not the framework itself.

body.speaker: Researcher A
body.text: Then we have a genuine disagreement about what constitutes the core intellectual contribution, and I think we need a third party to mediate. I'm not willing to submit with an authorship order we haven't agreed on.

body.speaker: Researcher B
body.text: I'm open to mediation, but I want to flag something first. We've spent three weeks on this discussion and the submission window closes in ten days. There is a real cost to delay — we could lose the journal slot entirely.

body.speaker: Researcher A
body.text: I understand that, and the timing is unfortunate. But publishing under an authorship order I believe is incorrect would follow me for the rest of my career. A journal slot matters for six months; authorship order on a significant paper matters permanently.

body.speaker: Researcher B
body.text: I take that point. What if we approached the department's research integrity office, not as a formal complaint but as a request for an advisory opinion? It's faster than mediation and it's less adversarial.

body.speaker: Researcher A
body.text: I can agree to that. I'd also want to propose that whatever the outcome, we append a contribution statement to the paper — the kind that specifies exactly who did what. That way the record is accurate regardless of the order.

body.speaker: Researcher B
body.text: That's something I can fully support. Contribution statements are increasingly standard and they serve precisely this function. Let's get the advisory opinion, and let's move quickly.

annotations.exampleEn: In many scientific fields, authorship order signals the relative contribution of each researcher.
The submission deadline was approaching, adding urgency to the authorship dispute.
Without the foundational work of earlier researchers, the current study would have been impossible.
The intellectual groundwork was laid years before the paper was written.

annotations.meaningVi: bài báo có thể công bố
annotations.exampleEn: Good data alone does not guarantee a publishable paper — framing and argument matter equally.

annotations.meaningVi: cách đóng khung được điều chỉnh lại
annotations.exampleEn: The revised framing made the research more relevant to the journal's thematic priorities.
Disputes about who designed the core intellectual framework are common in collaborative research.

annotations.meaningVi: bên thứ ba để hoà giải
annotations.exampleEn: When two researchers cannot agree, they may request a third party to mediate.

annotations.meaningVi: cửa sổ nộp bài; thời hạn nộp bài
annotations.exampleEn: The submission window for the special issue closes at the end of the month.

annotations.meaningVi: đối lập; mang tính đối kháng
annotations.exampleEn: A request for advisory opinion is less adversarial than filing a formal complaint.

annotations.meaningVi: văn phòng toàn vẹn nghiên cứu
annotations.exampleEn: Most universities have a research integrity office that handles disputes about attribution and ethics.
The researchers sought an advisory opinion rather than a formal ruling on the authorship question.
A contribution statement in a published paper specifies exactly what each author contributed.

annotations.meaningVi: hồ sơ/tài liệu là chính xác
annotations.exampleEn: Even if the order is disputed, a contribution statement ensures the record is accurate.

annotations.meaningVi: bất kể thứ tự như thế nào
annotations.exampleEn: Her contribution would be documented regardless of the order in which names appeared.
Missing the deadline would mean losing the journal slot to another submission.

annotations.meaningVi: hình thành khung trí tuệ cốt lõi
annotations.exampleEn: The researcher who conceived the core intellectual framework is generally listed as first author.
There is ongoing debate about what constitutes sufficient contribution for authorship.
The three-way comparison was the novel analytical design that made the paper publishable.
By repositioning the argument for a clinical readership, the researcher made the paper accessible to a wider audience.

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for completed actions with current relevance
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thì hiện tại hoàn thành (present perfect) được dùng để nối hành động đã hoàn thành trong quá khứ với hậu quả hiện tại. 'I developed it over the two years before you joined' (simple past — xác định thời gian) versus 'We've spent three weeks on this discussion' (present perfect — nhấn mạnh kết quả tích lũy ảnh hưởng đến hiện tại). 'I've read those guidelines too, and I read them differently' — 'I've read' xác nhận hành động đã xảy ra trong quá khứ không xác định; 'I read them' tiếp tục bằng simple past để mô tả cách đọc. Sự phân biệt này thường rất tinh tế ở trình độ B2.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Present perfect connects completed past actions to current relevance. 'We've spent three weeks on this discussion' — the accumulated time is a present constraint. 'I've read those guidelines too' — reading done at an unspecified past time is now relevant. Contrast with simple past: 'I developed it over the two years before you joined' — specific time reference. The distinction matters for argument: present perfect asserts a current state ('I have read and therefore I know'), while simple past narrates an event ('I developed it at a specific period').

grammarNotes.title: Nuanced modal distinctions: 'what constitutes' and 'I'm not willing to'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What constitutes X' là cấu trúc trang trọng hơn 'what counts as X' hay 'what is X', thường dùng trong ngữ cảnh pháp lý, học thuật hay đạo đức để nhấn mạnh tiêu chuẩn cần đáp ứng. 'I'm not willing to submit...' mạnh hơn 'I won't submit' — nó nhấn mạnh đây là quyết định có chủ đích, không phải sự bất lực. 'I'm open to mediation' (modal-like adjective) — 'open to' diễn đạt sự sẵn sàng mà không cam kết. Những sắc thái này quan trọng trong đàm phán ở trình độ B2.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What constitutes X' is more formal than 'what counts as X', typical in legal, academic, and ethical discourse where meeting a standard is at issue. 'I'm not willing to submit' is stronger than 'I won't submit' — it asserts deliberate choice, not inability. 'I'm open to mediation' uses an adjectival construction ('open to') to signal conditional willingness without full commitment. These distinctions are characteristic of careful, professional negotiation at B2–C1 level.

questions.prompt: What is Researcher A's core complaint?
options: Researcher B has not completed their section of the paper
Researcher A cannot be listed as second author because the methodology the paper presents was hers
The paper should not be submitted to this journal
Researcher B joined the project without proper agreement
questions.explanation: She says 'I can't be listed as second author. The methodology you're presenting was mine. I developed it over the two years before you joined.'
questions.hint: Listen for the specific authorship objection and its basis.

questions.prompt: How does Researcher B justify the proposed authorship order?
options: He argues that the person who joined the project first should be listed first
He argues that authorship order reflects contribution to the paper as submitted, not project history
He argues that the journal sets authorship order, not the researchers
He argues that all contributors should be listed alphabetically
questions.explanation: He says 'authorship order in our field reflects contribution to the paper as submitted, not to the project history.'
questions.hint: Listen for Researcher B's field convention argument about what authorship order reflects.

questions.prompt: What specific contributions does Researcher B say justify his first-author position?
options: He collected all the original data
He wrote the introduction and literature review
He developed three additional models, the revised framing, and the discussion section
He submitted the paper and managed the review process
questions.explanation: He says 'The analysis in the final draft — the three additional models, the revised framing, the discussion section — those were my work.'
questions.hint: Listen for the three-part list Researcher B gives of his contributions.

questions.prompt: What is Researcher A's counter-argument about visible versus foundational contributions?
options: She says writing is more important than methodology
She argues that Researcher B's framing only made sense because of her methodology underneath it
She says the methodology was not original but based on existing literature
She argues that all contributions are equal and order should be alphabetical
questions.explanation: She says 'That framing only made sense because of the methodology underneath it. If you remove my foundational work, there is no paper.'
questions.hint: Listen for what Researcher A says would happen if her foundational work were removed.

questions.prompt: What does Researcher B say the methodology was unable to do for two years before he joined?
options: It could not attract funding for further research
It had not produced a publishable paper
It had not been peer-reviewed
It had not been applied to real data
questions.explanation: He says 'the methodology had existed for two years without producing a publishable paper.'
questions.hint: Listen for what Researcher B says the methodology failed to produce despite two years of existence.

questions.prompt: What institutional guidelines does Researcher A invoke to support her position?
options: The journal's submission guidelines on authorship
The institution's authorship guidelines, which list the person who conceived the core intellectual framework first
International academic publishing standards
The department's internal peer review procedure
questions.explanation: She says 'In our institution's authorship guidelines, the person who conceived the core intellectual framework is listed first, regardless of who wrote the most in the final document.'
questions.hint: Listen for the specific institutional authority Researcher A cites.

questions.prompt: How does Researcher B interpret the same guidelines differently?
options: He says the guidelines do not apply to multi-disciplinary research
He says the 'core intellectual framework' refers to the three-way comparison he designed, not the original methodology
He says the guidelines were written before modern collaborative research practices
He says the guidelines only apply to single-authored papers
questions.explanation: He says 'The 'core intellectual framework' of this paper is the three-way comparison I designed. The original methodology is a tool within that framework, not the framework itself.'
questions.hint: Listen for how Researcher B redefines 'core intellectual framework' in this paper's context.

questions.prompt: What solution does Researcher A propose when they reach a genuine impasse?
options: Withdrawing the paper entirely
Listing both researchers as co-first authors
A third-party mediator
Asking the journal editor to decide
questions.explanation: She says 'we have a genuine disagreement... and I think we need a third party to mediate.'
questions.hint: Listen for what Researcher A explicitly proposes when they cannot agree.

questions.prompt: What concern does Researcher B raise about the timing of the dispute?
options: That they have already spent three weeks on the discussion and the submission window closes in ten days
That the journal has already assigned reviewers
That other researchers will learn about the dispute
That the department head will need to be informed
questions.explanation: He says 'We've spent three weeks on this discussion and the submission window closes in ten days. There is a real cost to delay — we could lose the journal slot entirely.'
questions.hint: Listen for the specific timeframes Researcher B mentions to illustrate the urgency.

questions.prompt: How does Researcher A respond to the time pressure argument?
options: She agrees to accept second authorship to avoid delay
She argues that a journal slot matters for six months but authorship order matters permanently
She proposes withdrawing from the paper entirely
She agrees to submit now and dispute authorship after publication
questions.explanation: She says 'A journal slot matters for six months; authorship order on a significant paper matters permanently.'
questions.hint: Listen for the specific time-contrast Researcher A uses to counter the urgency argument.

questions.prompt: What alternative to formal mediation does Researcher B propose?
options: Asking a senior colleague to decide informally
Consulting the journal editor
Approaching the research integrity office for an advisory opinion
Splitting the paper into two separate publications
questions.explanation: He says 'What if we approached the department's research integrity office, not as a formal complaint but as a request for an advisory opinion?'
questions.hint: Listen for the specific body Researcher B proposes consulting and the framing of the request.

questions.prompt: What additional solution does Researcher A propose to ensure accurate records?
options: Publishing an erratum after the paper is accepted
Including an acknowledgements section with detailed credits
Appending a contribution statement that specifies exactly who did what
Filing a formal record with the institution's library
questions.explanation: She says 'I'd also want to propose that whatever the outcome, we append a contribution statement to the paper — the kind that specifies exactly who did what.'
questions.hint: Listen for the specific document Researcher A proposes adding to the paper.

questions.prompt: What does Researcher B say about contribution statements?
options: They are controversial and rarely used
They are increasingly standard and serve precisely the function of ensuring accuracy
They can only be added after peer review
They are only required for papers with more than three authors
questions.explanation: He says 'Contribution statements are increasingly standard and they serve precisely this function.'
questions.hint: Listen for Researcher B's characterisation of contribution statements as a practice.

questions.prompt: What does the term 'advisory opinion' mean in this context?
options: A formal legal ruling by an external court
A non-binding recommendation from a relevant authority that helps the parties make a decision
Advice from a colleague outside the institution
A binding decision by the department head
questions.explanation: Researcher B contrasts it with 'a formal complaint' and says it is 'faster than mediation and less adversarial' — implying it is a non-binding expert view.
questions.hint: Listen for how Researcher B distinguishes this approach from a formal complaint and mediation.

questions.prompt: What is the broader issue at stake in this dialogue, beyond the practical authorship question?
options: Which researcher has more papers published in high-impact journals
The definition of intellectual contribution — whether foundational methodological work or final analytical synthesis deserves primary recognition
The speed of the institutional review process
The quality of the journal being targeted
questions.explanation: The dispute is ultimately about what kind of intellectual contribution is more fundamental — original methodology vs. analytical framing — which is a question without a clear universal answer.
questions.hint: Think about what each researcher is claiming as their primary contribution and what principle underlies each claim.

questions.prompt: Why does Researcher A say she is 'not willing' (rather than 'unable') to submit with the disputed authorship order?
options: She lacks the technical access to submit the paper
She is expressing deliberate refusal based on principle, not inability
She is waiting for the journal to confirm receipt of the manuscript
She cannot act without Researcher B's formal approval
questions.explanation: 'I'm not willing to' signals a principled refusal — she could submit but chooses not to. This is a more assertive stance than inability.
questions.hint: Think about the difference between 'I can't' (inability) and 'I'm not willing to' (deliberate choice).

cloze.template:
Researcher A: I need to raise this now, before the submission deadline. The {{b1}} order on the paper — I can't be listed as second author. The methodology you're presenting was mine. I developed it over the two years before you joined the project.

Researcher B: I understand why you feel that way. But authorship order in our field reflects contribution to the paper as {{b2}}, not to the project history. The analysis in the final draft — the three additional models, the revised framing, the discussion section — those were my work. That's the contribution that matters for publication.

Researcher A: That framing only made sense because of the methodology underneath it. If you remove my {{b3}} work, there is no paper. You're treating the visible contribution — the writing, the reframing — as if it's more significant than the intellectual groundwork that made it possible.

Researcher B: I'm not dismissing the foundational work. But the methodology had existed for two years without producing a {{b4}} paper. What turned it into one was the analytical approach I developed and the way I repositioned the argument for the journal. That's not a minor editorial contribution.

Researcher A: I accept that your contribution to the final draft was substantial. What I'm contesting is the principle. In our institution's authorship guidelines, the person who conceived the core intellectual {{b5}} is listed first, regardless of who wrote the most in the final document.

Researcher B: I've read those guidelines too, and I read them differently. The 'core intellectual framework' of this paper is the three-way comparison I designed. The original methodology is a {{b6}} within that framework, not the framework itself.

Researcher A: Then we have a genuine disagreement about what constitutes the core intellectual contribution, and I think we need a third party to {{b7}}. I'm not willing to submit with an authorship order we haven't agreed on.

Researcher B: I'm open to mediation, but I want to flag something first. We've spent three weeks on this discussion and the submission window closes in ten days. There is a real cost to delay — we could lose the journal {{b8}} entirely.

Researcher A: I understand that, and the timing is unfortunate. But publishing under an authorship order I believe is incorrect would follow me for the rest of my career. A journal slot matters for six months; authorship order on a significant paper matters {{b9}}.

Researcher B: I take that point. What if we approached the department's research {{b10}} office, not as a formal complaint but as a request for an advisory opinion? It's faster than mediation and it's less adversarial.

Researcher A: I can agree to that. I'd also want to propose that whatever the outcome, we append a contribution {{b11}} to the paper — the kind that specifies exactly who did what. That way the record is accurate regardless of the order.

Researcher B: That's something I can fully support. Contribution statements are increasingly standard and they serve precisely this function. Let's get the advisory opinion, and let's move quickly.

author

naming
blanks.explanation: 'Authorship order' — the sequence in which authors are listed on an academic publication.

options: submitted
published
written
presented
blanks.explanation: Authorship reflects contribution to the paper 'as submitted' — the final version, not historical contributions.

options: foundational
original
early
prior
blanks.explanation: 'Foundational work' — the basic work that everything else is built upon.

options: publishable
complete
finished
significant
blanks.explanation: A 'publishable paper' meets the standards required for academic journal publication.

options: framework
concept
contribution
methodology
blanks.explanation: The 'core intellectual framework' is the central organisational concept of the research.

options: tool
method
component
element
blanks.explanation: Researcher B calls the original methodology 'a tool within that framework' — useful but not the framework itself.

options: mediate
decide
arbitrate
resolve
blanks.explanation: 'A third party to mediate' — a neutral outsider to help resolve the dispute.

options: slot
position
space
chance
blanks.explanation: A 'journal slot' — the allocation of space in a specific journal issue.

options: permanently
forever
always
indefinitely
blanks.explanation: 'Matters permanently' — the authorship record will last as long as the paper is cited.

options: integrity
ethics
compliance
governance
blanks.explanation: The 'research integrity office' handles disputes about academic attribution and conduct.

options: statement
list
section
summary
blanks.explanation: A 'contribution statement' documents precisely what each author contributed to a paper.

translationVi: Hai nhà nghiên cứu tranh luận về thứ tự tác giả trên một bài báo sắp nộp. Nhà nghiên cứu A khẳng định rằng phương pháp luận mà bài báo trình bày là do cô phát triển trong hai năm trước khi nhà nghiên cứu B tham gia dự án. Nhà nghiên cứu B lập luận rằng thứ tự tác giả phản ánh đóng góp cho bài báo được nộp, không phải lịch sử dự án — và các mô hình phân tích, cách đóng khung, phần thảo luận là của anh. Nhà nghiên cứu A thách thức điều này, lập luận rằng cách đóng khung chỉ có ý nghĩa nhờ phương pháp luận bên dưới. Hai người bất đồng về cách diễn giải hướng dẫn tác giả của tổ chức. Sau khi không đạt được sự đồng thuận, nhà nghiên cứu A từ chối nộp bài với thứ tự tác giả chưa được thống nhất. Họ đồng ý tham khảo ý kiến từ văn phòng toàn vẹn nghiên cứu và thêm tuyên bố đóng góp vào bài báo để đảm bảo hồ sơ chính xác bất kể kết quả thế nào.
criticalThinkingQuestion: Both researchers make legitimate arguments: Researcher A emphasises the foundational intellectual work that made the paper possible; Researcher B emphasises the analytical and argumentative work that made it publishable. In collaborative research, is there a principled way to rank these types of contribution, or is authorship order inevitably a negotiation shaped by power, institutional norms, and timing? Should academic fields move away from single-sequence authorship toward contribution statements as the primary form of credit? What would be lost and gained by doing so?
